title: Dog Jelly-like Bloody Stool: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog passing jelly-like blood? Learn about HGE, why it is a high-priority emergency, and how to help your vet with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Jelly-like bloody stool is a high-priority medical emergency often caused by Acute Hemorrhagic Diarrhea Syndrome (AHDS), which requires immediate veterinary care to prevent life-threatening dehydration.

What causes my dog to have jelly-like bloody stool?

Seeing stool that resembles raspberry jam or has a jelly-like bloody consistency is a common sign of inflammation in the intestinal lining. This appearance is usually caused by a mixture of blood and mucus. In many cases, this is a symptom of Hemorrhagic Gastroenteritis (HGE), also known as Acute Hemorrhagic Diarrhea Syndrome (AHDS). It occurs when the lining of the gut becomes so irritated that it leaks fluid and blood into the digestive tract.

Is jelly-like bloody stool in dogs a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This should be treated as a medical emergency due to the rapid onset of dehydration and hypovolemic shock.
  • While causes range from dietary indiscretion to severe infection, dogs can lose a significant amount of fluid and protein through their bowels in a very short period.
  • If your dog is also lethargic, vomiting, or has a painful abdomen, they need to be seen by a veterinarian immediately.

How does taking a photo of the stool help the veterinary triage team?

  • A clear photo allows the triage team to quickly assess the severity of the bleeding and the ratio of mucus to blood.
  • The specific shade of red helps the veterinarian differentiate between minor colon irritation and life-threatening emergencies like AHDS.
  • Visual documentation ensures your dog receives the correct level of priority and care upon arrival at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

An acute onset of profuse hemorrhagic diarrhea, often described as resembling raspberry jam, is characteristic of Hemorrhagic Gastroenteritis (HGE) in small or toy breed dogs. Other common clinical signs include vomiting, anorexia, lethargy, and abdominal pain, with vomiting potentially preceding the onset of bloody diarrhea. Marked fluid loss can rapidly lead to hypovolemic shock. Diagnosis is typically based on signalment, acute clinical signs, hemoconcentration (PCV ≥55%), and normal to slightly decreased total plasma protein concentration. While selective fecal culture for pathogens like Clostridium spp., Salmonella spp., and Campylobacter spp., as well as fecal ELISA for Clostridium spp. enterotoxin, can be considered, abnormalities on CBC are usually limited to hemoconcentration and neutrophilic leukocytosis. Neutropenia, if present, raises concern for sepsis and/or parvovirus enteritis. Rectal polyps can also cause hematochezia and diarrhea. Rectal prolapse, especially in young animals with severe diarrhea and tenesmus, can involve protrusion of rectal layers through the anus.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 396)

faqs.question: Can I try a bland diet at home first?
faqs.answer: If there is significant blood or a jelly-like consistency, you should not wait. A bland diet is for mild upset stomach; bloody stool requires professional diagnostics to rule out life-threatening dehydration.

faqs.question: Could this be caused by something my dog ate?
faqs.answer: Yes, dietary indiscretion (eating trash or toxic foods) is a common cause, but it can also be caused by stress, parasites, bacteria, or parvovirus.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to treat this?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves aggressive intravenous (IV) fluid therapy to combat dehydration, along with anti-nausea medications and sometimes antibiotics or probiotics.
